question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Medium: A bug in the respective layer 0/1/2 network code that results in unintended smart contract behavior with no concrete funds at direct risk'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where two concurrent threads both call findStorageBatch() for the same contractId but different slot keys, both read the same cachedSlotKeys snapshot (which includes both keys), both execute findStorageBatch() with the same slot list, and both call contractStateCache.put() for the same slots — where the second put() overwrites the first with a value from a slightly later DB read, causing one thread to return a value that was cached by the other thread's DB query rather than its own?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Medium: A bug in the respective layer 0/1/2 network code that results in unintended smart contract behavior with no concrete funds at direct risk'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contractSlotsCache stores per-contract CaffeineCache instances as values, and that these nested cache instances are not subject to the contractSlotsCache's own eviction policy for their internal entries, so that even after the contractSlotsCache entry for a contract is evicted (due to maximumSize=3000), the per-contract cache object still exists in memory and is re-registered under a new contractSlotsCache entry, accumulating stale slot keys indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateServiceImpl.java -> Scope: Medium: A bug in the respective layer 0/1/2 network code that results in unintended smart contract behavior with no concrete funds at direct risk'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 2-second contractStateCache TTL to perform a time-of-check-time-of-use attack on a contract's storage: first call findStorage() to cache a slot value, then wait 2 seconds for the cache to expire, then trigger a contract state change in the underlying DB (via a real transaction), then call findStorage() again to get the new value — but if the EVM simulation spans the cache expiry boundary, different SLOAD opcodes for the same slot within one simulation may return different values?
